chapter_summary: The frame-story that opens the Mahabharata's recitation. In the Naimisha forest, at the twelve-year sacrificial session (satra) of the hermitage-chief Shaunaka, the bard Ugrashravas — son of Lomaharshana, a Suta and reciter of the Puranas — comes before the assembled sages. With folded hands he asks what they wish to hear. The sages reply that they will ask him the highest tales and he should narrate the connected story (katha-yoga), but first the venerable Shaunaka, who knows the divine tales of gods, asuras, men, serpents and gandharvas and who is a learned, truthful, vow-keeping ascetic and guru of the Aranyaka lore, must be awaited because he is occupied at the fire-sanctuary. They instruct the bard to begin only once Shaunaka is seated on the place of honor and asks him. The Suta agrees. Shaunaka, having completed his rites in order — gratifying the gods with words and the ancestors with water — returns to the sacrificial ground where the brahmin-seers and siddhas are seated, led by the Suta's son, and takes his seat among the priests and assembly-members. Seated among the seated, Shaunaka then begins to speak.

story.title: The Bard Arrives at Naimisha
story.arc: A storyteller seeks his audience and is told to wait for its master. Ugrashravas the Suta arrives at Shaunaka's twelve-year sacrifice and humbly offers to recite. The sages defer the choosing of the tale to their chief Shaunaka, who is still at the fire-altar, and bid the bard hold until the guru is seated and asks him himself. Shaunaka finishes his offerings to gods and ancestors, returns, and takes his seat among the priests — setting the stage for the great recitation to begin. The arc is pure threshold: anticipation deferred until the proper authority is present and honored.
story.timeline_note: Outer frame of the epic, set during Shaunaka's twelve-year satra in the Naimisha forest. This is the recitation-frame that wraps the entire Mahabharata; the events narrated within it are recalled, not happening now.
story.comic_potential: The polite stall: the eager bard shows up ready to perform, hands folded, only to be told 'great, but the boss isn't here yet — wait.' The whole assembly of ascetics marking time while one man finishes his chores at the fire-altar before anyone is allowed to enjoy the show.

teachings.teaching: Sacred knowledge is offered in humility, not asserted. The bard comes with folded hands and asks what the listeners wish to hear before he speaks a word — the teller places himself below the teaching he carries.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework this is the disciple's posture: realization is received, never seized. The folded hands before speaking mirror the surrender (the bowed attention) that precedes any true transmission — one empties oneself first, then the knowing flows.

teachings.teaching: The tale is not begun until the rightful guru is seated and asks for it. The sages withhold the narration, insisting the bard wait for Shaunaka — the one who is honored, truthful, vow-keeping and master of the lore — to take the seat of honor and pose the question.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma holds that Kriya passes only through the living guru in the proper order; knowledge does not pour out on demand but waits on the qualified seat. The teaching 'find your answer only when the guru asks the question' is the lineage-discipline at the heart of his Yoga — the practice finds you when the Guru finds you.

teachings.teaching: Right action is completed in order before the higher work begins. Shaunaka does not rush to the recitation; he first discharges every duty in sequence, satisfying the gods with words and the ancestors with water, and only then returns to his seat.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the outer discipline (kriya as 'action done rightly') is the prerequisite ground of the inner ascent: one settles the debts to gods and forefathers — purifies and orders the field — before sitting for the deeper knowing. Preparation in correct sequence is itself part of the sadhana, not a delay from it.
